name: The Commercial Hotel Bar, Market Street
description: The ground-floor saloon of Sandusky's principal commercial hotel, where traveling men, railroad contractors, and merchants drink in the amber light of six oil lamps. Arden Gill has a room upstairs and is currently at the corner table with a glass of rye whiskey and a commercial codebook open in front of him.
atmosphericDetails: Arden Gill's commercial codebook is open on the table but he is not writing in it — he is watching the door with the patient attention of a man who is waiting for something that should have happened already.
Silas Pratt came in twenty minutes ago, had one drink, spoke to Gill for less than two minutes, and left — the bartender noticed but did not think much of it.
The hotel's front window faces Market Street and the corner of Columbus Avenue; from this table, a man can see the door of the sheriff's office.
A folded piece of paper under Gill's whiskey glass, when unfolded, proves to be a rail schedule for the 6 a.m. southbound consist to Pittsburgh.
